spec_match.rationale: Bypasses the actual sibling-index() and sibling-count() APIs in active CSS by computing indices in JavaScript and injecting them inline.

interactive.rationale: Highly interactive with multiple project presets and custom controls for modifying the track list.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Concept is excellent, but the blurb claims 'No JavaScript colour math' when in fact JS is computing the sibling counts and indices.

edge_cases.rationale: Uses CSS.supports to probe tree-counting functions on load.

references.rationale: Omit the mandatory chromestatus.com/feature/<id> spec reference link.

design_system.rationale: Fully consistent with the project design variables, layouts, and tables.

openQuestions.title: Refactor to use CSS sibling-index() and sibling-count() instead of JS variables
openQuestions.detail: The timeline-chart currently bypasses tree-counting CSS functions in the active stylesheets by injecting --si and --sc via JavaScript. Refactor this by declaring `--si: sibling-index()` and `--sc: sibling-count()` directly on `li.track` (which are siblings under `ul.track-list`), so that `.track-bar` can inherit them, making the color-math fully CSS-driven without JS assistance.

openQuestions.title: Add mandatory chromestatus.com spec reference link
openQuestions.detail: The page is missing the mandatory reference link to the ChromeStatus entry. Please add a references section containing a link to `https://chromestatus.com/feature/6225478530367488`.

summary: A beautifully structured Gantt chart demo that claims to showcase sibling tree-counting functions. However, it is a complete simulation—the CSS variables are computed and injected via JavaScript, and it omits the mandatory ChromeStatus references.
